query: What specific Total Allowable Catch quotas has ICCAT set for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna for each year from 2022 to 2026, and what were the scientific basis and stock assessment results behind each decision?
synthesis:
ICCAT set the **Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna TAC at 40,570 t for 2022? No — for 2023–2025** under its adopted management procedure; therefore the TAC applied in **2022 was not established by these later recommendations**, while **2023, 2024, and 2025 each had a TAC of 40,570 t** inclusive of dead discards. The same recommendation states that **TACs for 2026 and thereafter would be decided at the 2025 annual meeting**. [24-05 BFT Recommendation](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2024-05-e.pdf) At the **2025 ICCAT Annual Meeting**, the Commission then set the **2026–2028 eastern Atlantic bluefin TAC at 48,403 t**, so the **2026 TAC is 48,403 t**. [ICCAT Press Release 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf)

Because your question asks for **each year from 2022 to 2026**, the year-by-year TACs that I could verify from ICCAT sources are:
- **2022:** I did **not find in the retrieved sources a recommendation text stating the 2022 eastern Atlantic/Mediterranean TAC**. The 2024 recommendation explicitly begins with **2023–2025 = 40,570 t**, implying 2022 was governed by an earlier measure not fully retrieved here. [24-05 BFT Recommendation](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2024-05-e.pdf)
- **2023:** **40,570 t**. [24-05 BFT Recommendation](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2024-05-e.pdf)
- **2024:** **40,570 t**. [24-05 BFT Recommendation](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2024-05-e.pdf)
- **2025:** **40,570 t**. [24-05 BFT Recommendation](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2024-05-e.pdf)
- **2026:** **48,403 t**. [ICCAT Press Release 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf)

## Scientific basis behind the 2023–2025 decision
The 2023–2025 TAC of **40,570 t** was set **“in accordance with the MP”** — ICCAT’s adopted **management procedure / harvest control rule** for Atlantic bluefin tuna. The recommendation expressly says the Commission had decided on the management procedure at its **2022 Annual Meeting** and that the eastern management plan starting in 2023 was based on that MP. [24-05 BFT Recommendation](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2024-05-e.pdf)

The scientific basis for that MP came from ICCAT’s **Management Strategy Evaluation (MSE)** work for Atlantic bluefin tuna, which ICCAT describes as having been under development for years and resulting in adoption of an Atlantic bluefin tuna MP in **2022 (Rec. 22-09)**; ICCAT’s MSE overview says TACs for both the eastern and western stocks have been set basically on this MP since 2022. [ICCAT Atlantic Bluefin Tuna MSE page](https://www.iccat.int/mse/en/specMSE_BFT.asp)

The most relevant stock assessment underpinning that shift was the **2022 eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean stock assessment**. That assessment used **three modelling platforms** — **VPA**, **Stock Synthesis**, and **ASAP** — and found a **sharp and steady increase in spawning stock biomass since about 2010** in the Stock Synthesis results, with improved retrospective behavior compared with the 2017 assessment. [SCRS/2022/013 Report of the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Stock Assessment meeting](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/CVSP/CV079_2022/n_3/CV079030426.pdf)

However, the 2022 assessment also showed **model disagreement on stock status relative to F0.1**. In the assessment summary, **VPA** indicated overfishing with **Fcurrent/F0.1 = 1.16** (95% CI **0.73–1.62**) under both recruitment scenarios, while **Stock Synthesis** estimated **Fcurrent/F0.1 = 0.72** (95% CI **0.62–0.80**) and **ASAP** estimated **0.54** (95% CI **0.48–0.60**), both indicating that overfishing was **not** occurring. [SCRS/2022/013 Report of the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Stock Assessment meeting](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/CVSP/CV079_2022/n_3/CV079030426.pdf)

The same assessment reported that estimated total biomass patterns were broadly similar between **Stock Synthesis and VPA in recent years**, while **ASAP indicated higher total biomass**, and it emphasized contradictory signals among data sources, including conflicts between **catch information, length composition, and abundance indices**. [SCRS/2022/013 Report of the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Stock Assessment meeting](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/CVSP/CV079_2022/n_3/CV079030426.pdf)

For MSE tuning, ICCAT’s 2022 assessment material shows that the MP/CMP evaluation used performance statistics such as probability of being in the **Kobe green quadrant**, probability of overfishing, biomass ratio after 20–30 years, and a proposed **Blim equal to 40% of dynamic SSBMSY** for CMP testing and tuning. [SCRS/2022/013 Report of the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Stock Assessment meeting](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/CVSP/CV079_2022/n_3/CV079030426.pdf)

So, for **2023**, **2024**, and **2025**, the specific TAC decision was not a fresh annual reassessment with a new quota each year; rather, ICCAT fixed **40,570 t for the whole 2023–2025 block**, based on the **newly adopted MP** and the **2022 assessment/MSE work** showing a rebuilt or rebuilding stock but with remaining uncertainty across assessment models. [24-05 BFT Recommendation](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2024-05-e.pdf) [SCRS/2022/013 Report of the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Stock Assessment meeting](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/CVSP/CV079_2022/n_3/CV079030426.pdf) [ICCAT Atlantic Bluefin Tuna MSE page](https://www.iccat.int/mse/en/specMSE_BFT.asp)

## Scientific basis behind the 2026 decision
For **2026**, ICCAT states that the new TACs for eastern and western bluefin were set **“based on the current management procedure”**. The Commission’s 2025 press release says the eastern TAC for **2026–2028** was fixed at **48,403 t**, a **19.3% increase** from the previous eastern TAC. [ICCAT Press Release 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf)

The 2025 SCRS report snippet available in search results says the **Bluefin Tuna Species Group’s main task in 2025 was to provide TAC advice for 2026–2028** and that the **adopted MP was used to calculate the 2026–2028 TACs** for the eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean management area. [SCRS 2025 report search result](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Reports/2025_SCRS_ENG.pdf)

ICCAT’s MSE overview further indicates that the Atlantic bluefin operating models were developed from prior stock assessments and that the Commission had adopted the MP in 2022, with TACs subsequently set on that basis; this supports the interpretation that the **2026 eastern TAC was generated by the MP rather than by a standalone conventional stock assessment TAC negotiation**. [ICCAT Atlantic Bluefin Tuna MSE page](https://www.iccat.int/mse/en/specMSE_BFT.asp)

## Important nuance on “stock assessment results behind each decision”
For **2023–2025**, the key stock assessment result I found is the **2022 eastern assessment**, whose central findings were: (1) **SSB had increased sharply since 2010** in Stock Synthesis; (2) **current overfishing status was uncertain across models**, with VPA above F0.1 but Stock Synthesis and ASAP below F0.1; and (3) the MP/MSE framework was designed and tuned using long-term performance statistics including Kobe green probability and a **Blim = 40% of dynamic SSBMSY** reference for testing. [SCRS/2022/013 Report of the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Stock Assessment meeting](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/CVSP/CV079_2022/n_3/CV079030426.pdf)

For **2026**, the sources I retrieved do **not** provide in-line detailed stock-status numbers analogous to the 2022 assessment; instead, they state that the TAC was derived from the **current management procedure** at the 2025 meeting. [ICCAT Press Release 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf) [SCRS 2025 report search result](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Reports/2025_SCRS_ENG.pdf)

## Bottom line
The verified eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna TACs I found are:
- **2023:** **40,570 t** [24-05 BFT Recommendation](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2024-05-e.pdf)
- **2024:** **40,570 t** [24-05 BFT Recommendation](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2024-05-e.pdf)
- **2025:** **40,570 t** [24-05 BFT Recommendation](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2024-05-e.pdf)
- **2026:** **48,403 t** [ICCAT Press Release 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf)

For **2022**, I was **not able to verify the specific TAC from the sources retrieved in this search**, so I cannot state it without risking fabrication. What I did find is that ICCAT’s major shift beginning in **2023** was to move to a **management-procedure-based quota of 40,570 t for 2023–2025**, grounded in the **2022 eastern stock assessment** and the broader **bluefin MSE process**. [24-05 BFT Recommendation](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2024-05-e.pdf) [SCRS/2022/013 Report of the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Stock Assessment meeting](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/CVSP/CV079_2022/n_3/CV079030426.pdf) [ICCAT Atlantic Bluefin Tuna MSE page](https://www.iccat.int/mse/en/specMSE_BFT.asp)

I found authoritative ICCAT materials for the **2023–2025** and **2026** TACs, plus the **2022 eastern stock assessment** details that explain the scientific basis for the 2023–2025 MP-based quota. I did **not** retrieve the earlier ICCAT recommendation text needed to verify the **2022 TAC**, and the retrieved 2025 materials did **not** expose detailed 2025 assessment metrics beyond saying the **current MP** was used to calculate the **2026–2028 TACs**.

sources.title: 24-05 BFT Recommendation
sources.content: Recommendation by ICCAT amending Recommendation 22-08 establishing a multi-annual management plan for bluefin tuna in the eastern Atlantic and the Mediterranean. Opening recitals acknowledge SCRS advice to move from the current recovery plan to a management plan; state that SCRS completed a management strategy evaluation (MSE) to establish a management procedure (MP) including harvest control rules; and say the Commission decided on the MP at its 2022 Annual Meeting to establish TACs for 2023 and thereafter. Paragraph 1 says CPCs shall implement a management plan for bluefin tuna in the eastern Atlantic and the Mediterranean starting in 2023 based on the MP in Rec. 23-07. Paragraph 4 states: TACs inclusive of dead discards for 2023 to 2025 shall be set at 40,570 t, in accordance with the MP. TACs for 2026 and thereafter shall be decided at the 2025 Commission Annual Meeting in accordance with the MP. It then gives annual quota allocations for 2023-2025 totaling 40,570 t, including EU 21,503 t, Morocco 3,700 t, Japan 3,114 t, Tunisia 3,000 t, Türkiye 2,600 t, Libya 2,548 t, Algeria 2,023 t, Egypt 513 t, Norway 368 t, Albania 264 t, Iceland 224 t, Korea 221 t, Syria 129 t, China 112 t, Chinese Taipei 101 t, UK 63 t, Namibia 50 t, plus 37 t unallocated reserve. It notes Mauritania and Senegal may each catch up to 5 t for research deducted from the reserve.

sources.title: SCRS/2022/013 Report of the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Stock Assessment meeting
sources.content: Report of the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Stock Assessment meeting (online, 4-9 July 2022). Assessment used three modelling platforms: VPA, Stock Synthesis, and ASAP. The report says the 2022 assessment shows improvement in SSB and recruitment retrospective analysis relative to 2017. Stock Synthesis section: model runs 1950-2020, fitted to length composition, age-at-length, 16 fishing fleets and 11 abundance indices; growth model was Richards with LINF fixed at 271 cm and K fixed at 0.23387; Beverton-Holt stock-recruit relationship with steepness 0.9 and sigmaR 0.6; diagnostics acceptable but important conflicts existed between catch information, length composition and index data. Stock Synthesis results showed SSB decreased from 1950 to the 1970s, remained relatively stable at low values during 1980-2009, and showed a sharp and steady increase since 2010. For stock status relative to F0.1, VPA estimated overfishing under both recruitment scenarios with Fcurrent(2017-2019)/F0.1 = 1.16 and 95% confidence intervals 0.73-1.62. Stock Synthesis estimated Fcurrent(2018-2020)/F0.1 = 0.72 (0.62-0.80) using exploitation-rate proxy, indicating overfishing not occurring. ASAP estimated Fcurrent(2018-2020)/F0.1 = 0.54 (0.48-0.60), also indicating overfishing not occurring. Estimates of total biomass in recent years were similar between Stock Synthesis and VPA, while ASAP indicated higher total biomass. The report says different models showed a relatively broad range of stock status relative to the F0.1 benchmark. The report also summarizes MSE/CMP work. Performance statistics included probability of being in Kobe green quadrant (PGK: SSB > dynamic SSBMSY and U < UMSY in year 30), biomass ratio after 20 and 30 years, probability of overfishing after 30 years, and probability of not being in the red Kobe quadrant. SCRS proposed a Blim of 40% of dynamic SSBMSY for CMP testing and tuning. The report also notes the Group recommended only projecting VPA for quantitative TAC advice for the Kobe 2 Strategy Matrix for continuity at that time.

sources.title: ICCAT Press Release 2025
sources.content: ICCAT press release on the 2025 Annual Meeting in Seville. States the meeting established conservation and management measures and new TACs and quotas for 2026-2028 for western and eastern bluefin tuna based on the current management procedures. Says full scientific stock assessments were carried out in 2025 for bigeye tuna, white marlin, and Atlantic shortfin mako shark. States: after long and intense discussions, ICCAT agreed to set new TACs for western and eastern bluefin tuna, based on the current management procedure, and new quota allocations. TACs for 2026-2028 were set at 3,081.6 t and 48,403 t for western and eastern Atlantic stocks, corresponding to TAC increases of 13% and 19.3%, respectively.

States Atlantic bluefin tuna has two stocks with mixing and that MSE has been under development since at least 2014. Says that following substantial efforts by SCRS and the Commission, a Management Procedure (MP) was adopted in 2022 (Rec. 22-09). States TACs for both western and eastern stocks have been set basically based on this MP since 2022. The page notes the need to conduct full stock assessments for eastern and western Atlantic bluefin tuna in 2017 (E/W), 2020 (E/W), 2021 (W), and 2022 (E). It provides links/references to SCRS reports and meeting documents for the MSE process and stock assessments.

States catches between 2018 and 2022 in the East Atlantic and Mediterranean were 27,782 t, 31,134 t, 35,038 t, 35,095 t, and 35,102 t, respectively; Mediterranean shares were 19,624 t, 22,090 t, 24,164 t, 24,786 t, and 24,625 t. States available information showed catches from the mid-1990s through 2007 were seriously under-reported, with realized total catch likely 50,000-61,000 t per year; since the 2017 assessment these estimates have been treated as actual catches. Also says the 2022 Stock Assessment meeting used ten abundance indices up to 2020 (seven CPUE series and three fisheries-independent indices), while the current MP uses five indices in each management area, and in the East these are two CPUE indices and three surveys.

query: What is the current deforestation rate in the Brazilian Cerrado biome, what are the main commodity drivers, and what specific enforcement actions has IBAMA taken in the last two years?
synthesis:
The Brazilian Cerrado has recently experienced a significant downward trend in deforestation after years of expansion, although it remains a major hotspot for vegetation loss. For the annual cycle of August 2024 to July 2025, the deforestation rate fell by 11.49%, with 7,235.27 km² cleared [In 2025, deforestation fell by 11.08 percent in the Amazon and by 11.49 percent in the Cerrado — Secretaria de Comunicação Social](https://www.gov.br/secom/en/latest-news/2025/10/in-2025-deforestation-fell-by-11-08-percent-in-the-amazon-and-by-11-49-percent-in-the-cerrado). This follows a 25.7% reduction recorded between 2023 and 2024 [Deforestation drops 77.2% in Pantanal, 57.2% in Cerrado between August-November 2024](https://www.gov.br/secom/en/latest-news/2024/12/deforestation-drops-77-2-in-pantanal-57-2-in-cerrado-between-august-november-2024). Despite these improvements, the Cerrado surpassed the Amazon as the most deforested biome in Brazil in 2024 [Deforestation for soy continues in Brazilian Cerrado despite EUDR looming](https://news.mongabay.com/2025/10/deforestation-for-soy-continues-in-brazilian-cerrado-despite-eudr-looming/), with 77.9% of the loss concentrated in the Matopiba region (Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, and Bahia) [In 2025, deforestation fell by 11.08 percent in the Amazon and by 11.49 percent in the Cerrado — Secretaria de Comunicação Social](https://www.gov.br/secom/en/latest-news/2025/10/in-2025-deforestation-fell-by-11-08-percent-in-the-amazon-and-by-11-49-percent-in-the-cerrado).

Soy production is the primary commodity driver of this loss, particularly in Western Bahia, where major producers like the Mizote Group and Franciosi Agro supply international traders Cargill and Bunge [Deforestation for soy continues in Brazilian Cerrado despite EUDR looming](https://news.mongabay.com/2025/10/deforestation-for-soy-continues-in-brazilian-cerrado-despite-eudr-looming/). In 2022 alone, soy was linked to 375,000 hectares of conversion in the biome [Brazilian soy exports and deforestation - Trase Insights](https://trase.earth/insights/brazilian-soy-exports-and-deforestation). Other drivers include beef production, mining, and financial speculation leading to land-grabbing [Deforestation drops 77.2% in Pantanal, 57.2% in Cerrado between August-November 2024](https://www.gov.br/secom/en/latest-news/2024/12/deforestation-drops-77-2-in-pantanal-57-2-in-cerrado-between-august-november-2024) [Financial Speculation Drives Massive Land Grabbing, Deforestation ...](https://www.actionaidusa.org/news/financial-speculation-drives-massive-land-grabbing-deforestation-in-brazils-cerrado/).

IBAMA and other federal agencies have intensified enforcement actions over the last two years through the "Action Plan for the Prevention and Control of Deforestation in the Cerrado" (PPCerrado). Specific actions include:
- **Operation Caixa-Forte (2025):** A targeted operation against financial institutions that granted rural credit to properties already under environmental embargo. Three banks in the Matopiba region were fined a total of R$ 3.63 million [The Liability of Financial Institutions in Brazil for Environmental Violations: Reflections from Operation Caixa-Forte](https://www.felsberg.com.br/en/the-liability-of-financial-institutions-in-brazil-for-environmental-violations-reflections-from-operation-caixa-forte/).
- **Increased Penalties:** Between 2023 and 2025, IBAMA saw a 130% increase in the value of fines issued and a 38% increase in environmental embargoes in the Cerrado [In 2025, deforestation fell by 11.08 percent in the Amazon and by 11.49 percent in the Cerrado — Secretaria de Comunicação Social](https://www.gov.br/secom/en/latest-news/2025/10/in-2025-deforestation-fell-by-11-08-percent-in-the-amazon-and-by-11-49-percent-in-the-cerrado).
- **Remote Enforcement:** Implementation of "Remote Embargoes," which allow agencies to restrict land use on illegally cleared areas using satellite data (DETER/PRODES) without requiring an in-person visit [MapBiomas Alerta - Reports and Law Enforcement Data](https://alerta.mapbiomas.org/en/relatorio/).
- **Joint Operations:** Collaborative efforts with the Federal Police and ICMBio, the latter of which conducted 91 operations resulting in 402 violation notices in the Cerrado between August 2024 and July 2025 [In 2025, deforestation fell by 11.08 percent in the Amazon and by 11.49 percent in the Cerrado — Secretaria de Comunicação Social](https://www.gov.br/secom/en/latest-news/2025/10/in-2025-deforestation-fell-by-11-08-percent-in-the-amazon-and-by-11-49-percent-in-the-cerrado).

sources.title: Deforestation for soy continues in Brazilian Cerrado despite EUDR looming
sources.content:
### Current Deforestation Rates in the Brazilian Cerrado
- **2024 Statistics:** The Cerrado lost **652,197 hectares (1.61 million acres)** of native vegetation in 2024, making it the most deforested biome in Brazil for the second consecutive year, surpassing even the Amazon.
- **Historical Expansion:** Agricultural activity in the Cerrado expanded by **74% between 1985 and 2024**.
- **Regional Hotspots:** The **Matopiba** region (straddling Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, and Bahia) accounted for **75% of all Cerrado deforestation** in 2024.
- **Current Status:** Just over half of the biome is still covered in native vegetation; however, in some municipalities, less than 20% of native vegetation remains.

### Main Commodity Drivers
- **Soy Production:** Soy is the primary driver of land clearing and agricultural expansion. 
- **Key Companies/Producers:** 
  - **Mizote Group and Franciosi Agro:** Cleared **986 hectares (2,436 acres)** since May 2024. Between January 2021 and April 2024, these two producers cleared a combined **23,164 hectares (57,239 acres)** in western Bahia.
  - **Horita Group:** Linked to properties with allegations of land grabbing and violence against local communities.
- **Supply Chain Links:** These producers sell soy to major commodity traders **Cargill and Bunge**, who are responsible for approximately **95% of soy exports** from western Bahia to the European Union (EU).
- **International Market Factors:** The European Union’s antideforestation regulation (EUDR) targets deforestation but currently excludes about **74% of the Cerrado** due to a narrow definition of "forest" that omits certain savannas and grasslands.

### Enforcement and Regulatory Actions
- **State Agency Involvement:** Earthsight identified deforestation permits granted by **Inema** (the Bahia state environmental agency), though it could not find permits for **60% of the cleared area** on certain properties, suggesting illegal clearing.
- **Certification Suspensions:** The **Roundtable on Responsible Soy (RTRS)** suspended the certificates of **Franciosi Agro and Horita Group** in 2024 following investigations into links between soy and deforested land. 
- **Policy Setbacks:** The article notes the potential **dismantling of the Soy Moratorium** (a voluntary agreement to curb soy-related deforestation) and a proposed one-year delay in the implementation of the EUDR.
- **Note on IBAMA:** While the article discusses state-level enforcement (Inema) and private certification suspensions (RTRS), it does not provide specific details on federal IBAMA enforcement actions (such as specific raid names or fine totals) from the last two years.

sources.title: Brazilian soy exports and deforestation - Trase Insights
sources.content:
### Cerrado Deforestation Rates and Drivers
*   **Deforestation Rate:** In 2022, soy plantations in the Brazilian Cerrado were associated with 375,000 hectares (ha) of deforestation and conversion. This area is more than twice the size of the city of São Paulo.
*   **Comparison with Other Biomes:** Soy production in the Amazon in 2022 was linked to 117,000 ha of deforestation, significantly lower than the Cerrado. The Pampas biome also saw high conversion, with 250,800 ha of soy harvested in recently converted areas.
*   **Commodity Drivers:** Soy expansion is the primary driver discussed. Beef is also identified as a major commodity linked to deforestation (with specific Trase analysis available for 2021-2023).
*   **Environmental Impact:** Deforestation for soy expansion in 2022 resulted in the release of 121 million tonnes of CO₂ (9% of Brazil's total land-use change emissions). The Cerrado accounted for 49 million tonnes of these emissions.

### Enforcement and Regulation Context
*   **Unauthorised Deforestation:** Recent analysis by Trase and Instituto Centro de Vida focuses on "unauthorised deforestation," using a new dataset on deforestation permits to help regulators and companies manage risks. 
*   **Enforcement Targeting:** The report suggests that the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) benchmarking system and subnational risk assessments can help competent authorities focus enforcement actions where they are most needed, particularly in regions where deforestation is concentrated.
*   **Zero-Deforestation Commitments (ZDCs):** In the Amazon, the Soy Moratorium is highly effective; soy production intensity there is 3.2 ha per 1,000 tonnes in covered areas versus 5 ha in non-covered areas. However, in the Cerrado, although 73% of soy is traded by companies with ZDCs, these commitments appear much less effective at preventing conversion.
*   **Supply Chain Transparency:** Approximately 15–18% of soy traded between 2020 and 2022 (around 20 million tonnes) cannot be linked to a specific municipality of production due to missing trade data and lack of information on trader silos or crushing facilities, creating challenges for enforcement and compliance.

sources.title: The Liability of Financial Institutions in Brazil for Environmental Violations: Reflections from Operation Caixa-Forte
sources.content:
### IBAMA Enforcement Actions (Cerrado Biome)
- **Operation Caixa-Forte**: Launched on March 19, 2025, this operation specifically targets financial institutions that granted rural credit to properties embargoed for illegal deforestation in the Cerrado biome.
- **Fines and Sanctions**: IBAMA has fined three banks located in the states of Maranhão, Tocantins, and Piauí, totaling more than R$ 3.63 million. Beyond financial penalties, these institutions face potential suspension or prohibition of new financing operations and the cancellation of environmental licenses.
- **Legal Basis**: The operation enforces regulations (referenced under Complementary Law No. 140, 2011) that prohibit the granting of rural credit to businesses on properties with environmental embargoes resulting from the economic use of illegally deforested areas. Areas must be registered in the 'Environmental Infractions and Embargoes Register' of IBAMA.
- **Data Sources for Enforcement**: IBAMA utilizes several databases for monitoring, including:
  * Rural property records (CAR).
  * Vegetation suppression authorizations.
  * Information on rural credit operations.

### Context on Deforestation and Drivers
- **Target Biome**: The enforcement focus is on illegal deforestation within the Cerrado (Brazilian Savanna).
- **Commodity Drivers**: While specific crop names are not explicitly listed, the enforcement focuses on 'rural credit' and 'economic use of illegally deforested areas' in the states of Maranhão, Tocantins, and Piauí (part of the Matopiba region), indicating that large-scale agricultural operations receiving financing are the primary drivers of the targeted illegal clearing.
- **Financial Liability**: Financial institutions are being held liable for non-compliance with environmental rules and may also be sanctioned by the Central Bank of Brazil.

sources.title: Deforestation drops 77.2% in Pantanal, 57.2% in Cerrado between August-November 2024
sources.content:
### Current Deforestation Rates in the Brazilian Cerrado
- **Recent Short-Term Trend:** Deforestation in the Cerrado decreased by 57.2% between August and November 2024 compared to the same period in 2023.
- **Annual Trend (PRODES/INPE):** The Cerrado biome saw a 25.7% reduction in deforestation between 2023 and 2024. 
- **Consistency:** Deforestation in the Cerrado has declined for nine consecutive months as of December 2024.
- **Broader Context:** In the Amazon, deforestation dropped 30.6% between August 2023 and July 2024 (6,288 km²), the lowest level in nine years.

### Main Drivers
While the source focuses on reduction statistics and policy, it identifies the following activities as targets of enforcement:
- **Illegal Deforestation:** Specifically targeted through command-and-control strategies.
- **Mining:** Listed as one of the primary criminal activities threatening Brazilian forests alongside illegal logging.
- **Economic Transition:** The government is promoting the "bioeconomy" and "sustainable productive activities" to replace current drivers and value "standing forests."

### Specific Enforcement and IBAMA Actions (Last 2 Years)
- **Action Plans:** Implementation of the "Action Plan for the Prevention and Control of Deforestation in the Cerrado" (PPCerrado). This plan includes environmental monitoring, land management, and economic regulatory instruments.
- **Inter-Agency Integration:** IBAMA has intensified joint operations with the Chico Mendes Institute for Biodiversity Conservation (ICMBio), the National Force, and the Federal Police.
- **Advanced Technology:** Enforcement now relies heavily on the use of drones, satellite monitoring (including INPE’s DETER system for real-time detection), and intelligence information sharing to provide prompt responses to environmental crimes.
- **Strategic Areas:** Actions are coordinated across four main pillars: environmental monitoring and control, land and territorial management, promoting sustainable productive activities, and economic/regulatory instruments.
- **Policy Goals:** The administration is utilizing these command-and-control strategies to work toward a target of zero deforestation in Brazil by 2030.

sources.title: Cerrado and Amazon deforestation drop in first half of 2024
sources.content:
According to data from the Real-Time Deforestation Detection System (DETER-B) operated by Brazil’s National Space Research Institute (INPE), deforestation in the Cerrado biome experienced its first decline since 2020 during the first half of 2024.

- **Current Deforestation Rates (Cerrado):**
  - **First Half of 2024 (Jan–June):** Deforestation alerts dropped by 15%, encompassing 3,724 km² compared to 4,396 km² in the same period of 2023.
  - **June 2024:** Deforestation decreased by 24% (662 km² down from 875 km² in June 2023).
  - **Annual Cycle (August 2023 to June 2024):** Despite the recent monthly drops, the cumulative area affected by alerts in this period totaled 6,571 km², a 16% increase compared to the 5,735 km² recorded in the previous equivalent cycle. This follows a major 43.6% increase in 2023 compared with 2022.

- **Monitoring and Enforcement Actions:**
  - **IBAMA and ICMBio:** The Brazilian Institute for the Environment and Renewable Natural Resources (IBAMA) and the Chico Mendes Institute for Biodiversity Conservation (ICMBio) utilize daily DETER alerts to conduct on-the-ground inspections. These daily alerts are designed to support immediate enforcement actions rather than just long-term monitoring.
  - **Strategy:** Minister Marina Silva attributed the downward trend to consistent government intervention, aiming for zero deforestation by 2030. The methodology involves using DETER for rapid response and PRODES (Legal Amazon Deforestation Satellite Monitoring Project) for precise annual satellite imagery (measured August to July).

- **Commodity Drivers:** The provided text does not explicitly name specific commodities (such as soy or cattle) as the primary drivers, but it highlights that the decline in the first half of 2024 marks a reversal of a multi-year growth trend in the biome.

sources.title: Quality assessment of the PRODES Cerrado deforestation data - ScienceDirect
sources.content:
The Cerrado, the second largest biome in Brazil and a global biodiversity hotspot, has been subject to official monitoring via the PRODES Cerrado initiative since 2016 (tracking suppression since 2000). Key findings regarding deforestation and land use in this biome include:

- **Deforestation Scale and Anthropization:** Sample-based validation indicates that the anthropization process (human transformation of the landscape) in the Cerrado surpassed 50% of the total biome area by 2013. However, official PRODES Cerrado data only identified this 50% threshold in 2018. 
- **Monitoring Accuracy:** The official PRODES Cerrado data has been validated with an overall accuracy of 93.17 ± 0.89%, making it a reliable instrument for territorial governance and environmental policy.
- **Commodity Drivers and Agricultural Expansion:** The study identifies soy as a critical factor, noting that the PRODES Cerrado data is adequate to support a 'soy-moratorium' in the biome. Agricultural production is the primary driver of land use change, and approximately 104,000 km² of abandoned land has been identified as available for agricultural expansion, which could potentially curb the need for new deforestation.
- **Governance and Policy:** The monitoring system is intended to support instruments capable of curbing or zeroing deforestation. The paper specifically highlights the role of the Forest Investment Program (FIP) in supporting these monitoring efforts.

[Note: This source is an academic abstract from 2020/2021; it does not contain real-time deforestation rates for 2023-2024 or specific IBAMA enforcement actions from the last two years.]

sources.title: MapBiomas Alerta - Reports and Law Enforcement Data
sources.content:
The MapBiomas Alerta platform provides annual reports (RAD) and specific dashboards tracking deforestation and enforcement actions across all Brazilian biomes, including the Cerrado. 

### Deforestation Reports (RAD 2022-2024):
- **RAD 2024 (Annual Report on Deforestation in Brazil):** Provides the most recent 'x-ray' of deforestation in all Brazilian biomes (2023-2024 data). The Factsheet contains the main data highlights from this report.
- **RAD 2023:** Presents a comprehensive analysis of deforestation across all biomes for the year 2023.
- **RAD 2022:** Details deforestation patterns and drivers for the year 2022.
- **Historical Data:** The RAD 2019 report noted a loss of at least 1.2 million hectares of native vegetation across Brazil.

### Law Enforcement and IBAMA Actions:
- **Law Enforcement Dashboard (Monitor da Fiscalização):** Tracks the progress of environmental inspection and enforcement actions. This dashboard is designed to monitor the fight against deforestation by public bodies, including IBAMA and state agencies.
- **Federal Government Action Report:** A report produced in partnership with the Democracy and Sustainability Institute (IDS) analyzes federal government actions (including IBAMA) specifically in relation to deforestation alerts. It provides evidence on the current situation of federal agency responses and transparency regarding requests sent to these agencies.
- **Remote Embargoes:** The platform includes a 'Good Practice Guide for Implementing the Remote Embargo in Deforested Areas in Brazil,' which is a specific enforcement tool used by environmental agencies like IBAMA to restrict activities in illegally cleared areas without the need for an in-person visit.
- **State-Level Data:** MapBiomas holds webinars and publishes reports on the fight against deforestation by public bodies in specific states (e.g., Regional Webinars Report), which include recommendations for environmental inspection agencies.

### Biome-Specific Context:
While specific percentages for the Cerrado are detailed within the PDF reports and dashboards linked on the page, the platform confirms that these reports provide a 'complete x-ray' of deforestation for the Cerrado biome alongside the Amazon, Atlantic Forest, Caatinga, Pampa, and Pantanal.

sources.title: In 2025, deforestation fell by 11.08 percent in the Amazon and by 11.49 percent in the Cerrado — Secretaria de Comunicação Social
sources.content:
### Current Deforestation Rate in the Brazilian Cerrado
- **Rate and Reduction:** Deforestation in the Cerrado fell by **11.49%** for the period of August 2024 to July 2025. This marks the second consecutive year of decline for the biome, following five years of increases between 2019 and 2023.
- **Area Deforested:** The official deforestation rate for this period was **7,235.27 km²**.
- **Geographic Concentration:** Approximately **77.9%** of the deforestation in the Cerrado occurred in the **Matopiba** region, which comprises the states of Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, and Bahia.
- **Environmental Impact:** The reduction in both the Amazon and Cerrado since 2022 has avoided an estimated 733.9 million tons of CO₂e emissions, roughly equivalent to the combined annual emissions of Spain and France in 2022.

### Main Commodity Drivers and Regional Context
- The text identifies the **Matopiba region** as the primary site of deforestation in the Cerrado (77.9% of the total). While the specific commodities (such as soy or cattle) are not explicitly named in this article, the region is noted for illegal deforestation, land invasion, and the illegal occupation of federal lands. 
- The government's strategy includes combating "illegal deforestation" and "forest degradation" through the Action Plan for the Prevention and Control of Deforestation in the Cerrado.

### IBAMA and ICMBio Enforcement Actions (2023–2025)
IBAMA (Brazilian Institute of the Environment and Renewable Natural Resources) and ICMBio have intensified inspection and monitoring efforts over the last two years:
- **Cerrado-Specific IBAMA Actions:**
  - **Violation Notices:** Increased by **24%** for flora-related offenses.
  - **Fines:** Rose by **130%**.
  - **Embargoes:** Increased by **38%**, with a **26% expansion** in the total embargoed area.
- **Amazon-Specific IBAMA Actions (for comparison):**
  - **Violation Notices:** Increased by 81%.
  - **Fines:** Rose by 63%.
  - **Embargoes:** Increased by 51%, with a 49% rise in embargoed area.
- **ICMBio Operations (August 2024 – July 2025):**
  - In the Cerrado: Conducted **91 operations**, resulting in **402 violation notices** and **218 embargoes**.
  - In the Amazon: Conducted 312 enforcement actions, resulting in 1,301 violation notices and 816 embargoes in federal conservation units.
- **Systemic Actions:**
  - Resumption of the **Amazon Fund**, with BRL 3.642 billion invested over the last three years and BRL 850 million specifically allocated to strengthen environmental enforcement.
  - Use of the **PRODES** (annual rate) and **DETER** (daily alerts) systems for satellite monitoring.
  - Reinstatement of the Permanent Interministerial Commission for the Prevention and Control of Deforestation and Fires (CIPPCD), involving 19 ministries.
  - Signing of an environmental emergency decree by the Ministry of Environment to address forest fire risks in vulnerable areas.

query: What is the current deforestation rate in the Brazilian Cerrado biome, what are the main commodity drivers, and what specific enforcement actions has IBAMA taken in the last two years?
synthesis:
The most current **official annual deforestation rate** for the Brazilian Cerrado biome I found is **7,235.27 km²** for **August 2024–July 2025**, according to IBAMA’s publication on the latest PRODES results released **October 30, 2025**. IBAMA says this was an **11.49% decline** from the prior annual period and the **second consecutive annual reduction** after five years of increases from 2019–2023. It also states that **77.9%** of Cerrado deforestation in that cycle occurred in **MATOPIBA** (Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, Bahia). [Em 2025, desmatamento tem redução de 11,08% na Amazônia e 11,49% no Cerrado](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/em-2025-desmatamento-tem-reducao-de-11-08-na-amazonia-e-11-49-no-cerrado)

For additional context, Brazil’s official NDC and federal communications report the previous annual Cerrado rate at **8,174 km²** for **August 2023–July 2024**, a **25.7% reduction** versus 2023. [BRAZIL’S NDC](https://www.unfccc.int/sites/default/files/2024-11/Brazil_Second%20Nationally%20Determined%20Contribution%20%28NDC%29_November2024.pdf) [In one year, deforestation and conversion falls 30.6% in the Amazon and 25.7% in the Cerrado | WWF Brasil](https://www.wwf.org.br/en/%3F90200/In-one-year-deforestation-falls-306-in-the-Amazon-and-257-in-the-Cerrado)

If by “current deforestation rate” you mean the most recent **calendar-year validated area** rather than the official PRODES annual cycle, IBAMA’s May 15, 2025 note citing **MapBiomas RAD 2024** reports **652,197 hectares** cleared in the Cerrado in **calendar year 2024**, equal to roughly **6,522 km²**, and says the biome still accounted for **52.5%** of all Brazilian deforestation that year. This is a different monitoring framework from PRODES, so it should not be treated as directly interchangeable with the PRODES annual rate. [Desmatamento no Brasil caiu 32,4% em 2024](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/desmatamento-no-brasil-caiu-32-4-em-2024)

On the **main commodity drivers**, the sources consistently point to **agricultural expansion** as the dominant proximate cause in the Cerrado, especially in MATOPIBA. MapBiomas-linked reporting says **agriculture was responsible for more than 97% of native vegetation loss in the last six years**, with the Maranhão Cerrado especially pressured by **large-scale monocultures of soy and corn**, plus **livestock** and some **mining**. [The Cerrado is the most deforested biome, despite a decrease indicated by RAD 2024 - ISPN - Institute for Society, Population and Nature.](https://ispn.org.br/en/noticia/cerrado-e-bioma-mais-desmatado-apesar-de-queda-apontada-pelo-rad-2024/)

More specifically, the commodity pattern across the sources is: **soy** is a major direct driver of Cerrado conversion; SEI/Trase says soy plantations expanded most in the Cerrado and were associated with **375,000 hectares of deforestation and conversion in 2022**. [Trase: Brazilian soy exports and deforestation | SEI](https://www.sei.org/features/brazilian-soy-exports-and-deforestation/)

**Cattle** is also a major driver and, in broader Brazil-wide supply-chain analysis, is described by Global Canopy as the **single most influential commodity for deforestation and associated greenhouse-gas emissions**; Global Canopy also says cattle together with agriculture such as soy can account for **up to 97% of deforestation and land conversion** in Brazil. [Brazilian beef exports and deforestation – Global Canopy](https://globalcanopy.org/insights/explainer/brazilian-beef-exports-and-deforestation/) [Roadmap – Deforestation-Free Finance](https://guidance.globalcanopy.org/roadmap/)

IBAMA’s own Cerrado enforcement note gives commodity-specific evidence from the ground: in Operation **Nova Fronteira**, inspectors found embargoed lands being used illicitly for the sale of **soybeans, corn, beans, and other crops**, as well as land sales and leasing. [Desmatamento ilegal no Cerrado gera R$ 206 milhões em multas por descumprimento de embargos e impedimento da regeneração natural — Ibama](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2024/desmatamento-ilegal-no-cerrado-gera-r-206-milhoes-em-multas-por-descumprimento-de-embargos-e-impedimento-da-regeneracao-natural)

On **specific IBAMA enforcement actions in the last two years** (roughly 2024–2025), the clearest Cerrado-specific actions I found are:

1. **Intensified inspection and embargoes in the Cerrado (Aug. 2023–Jul. 2024)**: IBAMA registered **733 infraction notices**, **449 embargoes**, and **124 seizure notices**, with **more than R$225 million in fines** during the period. The federal government’s NDC also says IBAMA increased use of **remote embargoes** for areas with illegal deforestation above **0.5 km² (50 ha)** in the Cerrado. [BRAZIL’S NDC](https://www.unfccc.int/sites/default/files/2024-11/Brazil_Second%20Nationally%20Determined%20Contribution%20%28NDC%29_November2024.pdf) [Dia do Cerrado: Governo Federal reforça combate ao desmatamento e proteção das bacias hidrográficas — Secretaria de Comunicação Social](https://www.gov.br/secom/pt-br/acompanhe-a-secom/noticias/2024/09/dia-do-cerrado-governo-federal-reforca-combate-ao-desmatamento-e-protecao-das-bacias-hidrograficas)

2. **Operation Nova Fronteira in MATOPIBA (Oct.–Nov. 2024)**: IBAMA says this operation against illegal Cerrado deforestation produced **52 enforcement actions/autuações**, about **R$206 million in environmental fines**, and the **seizure of 23 pieces of equipment and machinery** such as tractors and sprayers. IBAMA says the operation targeted **noncompliance with prior embargoes** and **obstruction of natural regeneration**. [Desmatamento ilegal no Cerrado gera R$ 206 milhões em multas por descumprimento de embargos e impedimento da regeneração natural — Ibama](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2024/desmatamento-ilegal-no-cerrado-gera-r-206-milhoes-em-multas-por-descumprimento-de-embargos-e-impedimento-da-regeneracao-natural)

3. **Broader Cerrado enforcement totals reported for Aug. 2024–May 2025**: IBAMA reported **R$578 million in fines**, **737 infraction notices**, **455 embargo orders**, **376 seizure notices** covering **R$54.9 million in seized goods**, **77 destruction notices**, **370 fire-prevention notifications**, and **20 civil public actions** in the Cerrado. [No mês de maio, Pantanal e Cerrado registram continuidade na queda no desmatamento; Amazônia é afetada por incêndios e seca histórica — Ibama](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/no-mes-de-maio-pantanal-e-cerrado-registram-continuidade-na-queda-no-desmatamento-amazonia-e-afetada-por-incendios-e-seca-historica)

4. **Structural enforcement changes supporting Cerrado control**: the federal government reports cooperation with **MATOPIBA states** to verify vegetation-suppression authorizations, inspections of **priority targets**, and integration of **SICAR** with the Central Bank system to help block rural credit for properties overlapping protected areas, Indigenous lands, and federal or state embargoes. [Dia do Cerrado: Governo Federal reforça combate ao desmatamento e proteção das bacias hidrográficas — Secretaria de Comunicação Social](https://www.gov.br/secom/pt-br/acompanhe-a-secom/noticias/2024/09/dia-do-cerrado-governo-federal-reforca-combate-ao-desmatamento-e-protecao-das-bacias-hidrograficas)

So, in short: the **current official annual PRODES deforestation rate for the Cerrado is 7,235.27 km² (Aug. 2024–Jul. 2025)**; the **main commodity drivers are agricultural expansion led by soy, corn, and cattle/livestock, concentrated in MATOPIBA**; and in the last two years IBAMA’s most clearly documented Cerrado actions include **remote embargoes, hundreds of infraction notices and embargoes, R$225 million+ in fines in 2023/24, Operation Nova Fronteira’s R$206 million in fines and 23 equipment seizures in late 2024, and by May 2025 a broader enforcement tally of R$578 million in fines plus embargoes, seizures, destruction notices, and civil actions**. [Em 2025, desmatamento tem redução de 11,08% na Amazônia e 11,49% no Cerrado](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/em-2025-desmatamento-tem-reducao-de-11-08-na-amazonia-e-11-49-no-cerrado) [Desmatamento ilegal no Cerrado gera R$ 206 milhões em multas por descumprimento de embargos e impedimento da regeneração natural — Ibama](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2024/desmatamento-ilegal-no-cerrado-gera-r-206-milhoes-em-multas-por-descumprimento-de-embargos-e-impedimento-da-regeneracao-natural) [No mês de maio, Pantanal e Cerrado registram continuidade na queda no desmatamento; Amazônia é afetada por incêndios e seca histórica — Ibama](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/no-mes-de-maio-pantanal-e-cerrado-registram-continuidade-na-queda-no-desmatamento-amazonia-e-afetada-por-incendios-e-seca-historica)

One caveat: the sources give **different “current” numbers depending on methodology and time window**. **PRODES** gives the official annual rate in km² for an **August–July cycle**; **MapBiomas RAD** gives validated **calendar-year hectares**. Both are useful, but they answer slightly different questions. [Desmatamento no Brasil caiu 32,4% em 2024](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/desmatamento-no-brasil-caiu-32-4-em-2024) [BRAZIL’S NDC](https://www.unfccc.int/sites/default/files/2024-11/Brazil_Second%20Nationally%20Determined%20Contribution%20%28NDC%29_November2024.pdf)

sources.title: BRAZIL’S NDC
sources.content: PDF. On pages 32–33, in section on PPCerrado, states: In 2024 Brazil achieved a 25.7% reduction in deforestation rates in the Brazilian Cerrado compared to 2023. Lists strategic actions: launch of PPCerrado in November 2023; increase in remote embargoes by IBAMA for areas with illegal deforestation above 0.5 km² (50 hectares), while average for biome is 5 km² (500 hectares); intensified inspection from Aug 2023 to Jul 2024 with 733 infraction notices, 449 embargoes, 124 seizure notices, and more than R$225 million in fines by IBAMA; ICMBio registered 85 infraction notices, 135 embargoes, 98 seizure notices, 28 destruction notices, and R$16 million in fines; creation of a task force with ministers and governors of Cerrado states; involvement of agribusiness leaders; actions to increase transparency and integration of Vegetation Suppression Authorization (ASV) data; definition of priority municipalities; stricter prohibition on rural credit in illegally deforested areas under CMN Resolution No. 5,081 of June 29, 2023.

sources.content: Published 02/12/2024. Operation Nova Fronteira targeted illegal deforestation in the Cerrado biome in MATOPIBA (parts of Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, Bahia). Resulted in 52 autuações/enforcement actions totaling approximately R$206 million in environmental fines during October and November. 23 pieces of equipment and machinery were seized, including tractors and sprayers. sources.title: Dia do Cerrado: Governo Federal reforça combate ao desmatamento e proteção das bacias hidrográficas — Secretaria de Comunicação Social
sources.content: Published September 2024. Says PPCerrado foresees coordination between federal government and states to contain devastation of the biome. Federal government working on high-level pact with Cerrado governors, formally proposed in March 2024, including creation of task force for joint actions, integration of databases, and definition of priority municipalities for control actions. Other measures include cooperation with MATOPIBA states to verify vegetation suppression authorizations and inspect areas with deforestation identified by MMA; inspections of priority targets with fines, embargoes and seizures by IBAMA and ICMBio; integration of SICAR with Central Bank systems to block credit operations on properties overlapping protected areas, Indigenous lands, and federal/state embargoes; and progress in integrating state deforestation data into Sinalfor. Also states that from Aug 2023 to Jul 2024, IBAMA in the Cerrado recorded 733 infraction notices, 449 embargoes, 124 seizure terms, and more than R$225 million in fines.

sources.title: Desmatamento no Brasil caiu 32,4% em 2024 — Ibama
sources.content: Published 15/05/2025. IBAMA note citing MapBiomas Alerta Annual Deforestation Report 2024. Says Brazil’s total deforested area in 2024 was 1,242,079 hectares, down 32.4% from 2023, with 60,983 validated alerts. Cerrado had the largest deforested area for second year running: 652,197 hectares in 2024, more than half (52.5%) of Brazil’s total. Says Cerrado deforestation fell 41.2% versus 2023. MATOPIBA concentrated 75% of Cerrado deforestation and about 42% of all native vegetation loss in Brazil. Maranhão led all states with 218,298.4 hectares deforested in 2024 despite a 34.3% reduction. Notes savanna formations accounted for 52.4% of all deforestation in Brazil.

sources.title: The Cerrado is the most deforested biome, despite a decrease indicated by RAD 2024 - ISPN - Institute for Society, Population and Nature.
sources.content: English article on RAD 2024 / MapBiomas findings. Says attention is drawn to continued leadership of MATOPIBA as frontier of agricultural expansion in suppression of native vegetation, especially in Cerrado. According to the report, agriculture is the main driver of deforestation, responsible for more than 97% of native vegetation loss in the last six years. Says destruction in Maranhão Cerrado is mainly driven by advance of large-scale monoculture with soy and corn, as well as livestock farming and mining. Presents these as main proximate drivers of Cerrado deforestation.

sources.title: Trase: Brazilian soy exports and deforestation | SEI
sources.content: SEI/Trase feature. States soy plantations have expanded most in the Cerrado and in 2022 were associated with 375,000 hectares of deforestation and conversion. Notes updated MapBiomas database used by Trase improves accounting of loss of other wooded lands and natural grasslands. Used here as evidence of soy as a major direct commodity driver in Cerrado.

sources.title: Brazilian beef exports and deforestation – Global Canopy
sources.content: Global Canopy explainer on Brazilian beef exports and deforestation. States cattle is highly influential in Brazilian deforestation and emissions. Notes satellite monitoring data for 2024 from INPE PRODES showed deforestation fell by 31% in the Amazon and 26% in the Cerrado. Gives market context such as China accounting for 59% of Brazilian beef exports in 2023, US 8%, EU 5%. Used primarily for claim that cattle is a major commodity driver.

sources.title: Roadmap – Deforestation-Free Finance
sources.content: Global Canopy guidance page. States cattle is the single most influential commodity globally for deforestation and linked greenhouse gas emissions. Says Brazil accounts for nearly 60% of global pasture-driven deforestation, and within Brazil the cattle supply chain, combined with agriculture such as soy production, is responsible for up to 97% of deforestation and land conversion. Used as broader commodity-driver context relevant to Cerrado commodity pressures.

sources.title: In one year, deforestation and conversion falls 30.6% in the Amazon and 25.7% in the Cerrado | WWF Brasil
sources.content: WWF Brasil article summarizing official data. Says Legal Amazon lost 6,288 km² between Aug 2023 and Jul 2024. States Cerrado had a significant reduction of 25.7% but remained at worrying levels, with a rate of 8,174 km². Used to corroborate prior official annual Cerrado rate for Aug 2023–Jul 2024.

evaluation.url_relevance_rationale: The sources are excellent, combining official government data from IBAMA and the Brazilian NDC with high-quality independent research from MapBiomas, Trase, and Global Canopy.
evaluation.tailored_comprehensiveness_rationale: The extracted content is exceptionally detailed, providing specific deforestation figures for multiple years, precise enforcement metrics (fines, notices, and equipment seized), and clear identification of commodity drivers.
evaluation.synthesis_quality_rationale: The synthesis is comprehensive and precise, clearly distinguishing between different monitoring cycles (PRODES vs. MapBiomas) and providing specific, attributed data for every part of the user's query.
